source_quote: We saw an unprecedented surge in interest in food growing when the pandemic started, with our customers and community reporting huge benefits to their mental health and overall well-being. Food growing has a unique way of connecting people with the planet and will be a powerful tool to mobilise positive environmental action in the years ahead. target_quote: The main challenge is sustaining engagement and ownership from players in their mental health journey. target_quote: Young people from a care-experienced background are more likely to experience poor mental health outcomes due to the trauma and instability they have faced in their lives. This has been further complicated by covid-19 and lockdowns which in many cases had a negative impact on relationships, prevented young people from socializing outside of the home, and increased mental health complaints such as anxiety and depression. On the other hand, young people are more likely than ever to be assessed for neurodivergence or learning difficulties, which allows them to gain access to support earlier.

source_quote:
The main focus of our programmes is on encouraging movement and getting people, particularly teenagers, active. The goal is to improve both mental and physical well-being by encouraging them to get off their phones and out of the house. This includes specific initiatives like the "Outdoor Week" and "Teenagers in Sports" week.
The funding for these programmes is allocated by Sport Ireland, which provides money that is then distributed to support the initiatives.
Overall, the programmes operate under the broader banners of well-being and inclusion, which also encompass other initiatives like "women in sports."
target_quote: The more important thing is the programs through the surf clubs, engagement, participation, getting people outside. It's not all about, you know, being a competitive surfer. It's that's actually the way smaller part of the picture. The main picture is the inclusion, getting people activated in the sport, and they don't have to take it up to a high level. If we can create lifelong participants, rather than competitive participants, would actually be our goal.
description: Sequential relation in channel 9511.0

source_quote: The more important thing is the programs through the surf clubs, engagement, participation, getting people outside. It's not all about, you know, being a competitive surfer. It's that's actually the way smaller part of the picture. The main picture is the inclusion, getting people activated in the sport, and they don't have to take it up to a high level. If we can create lifelong participants, rather than competitive participants, would actually be our goal.
target_quote:
The main challenge is always money. We need more funding to run more programs and increase participation. This would allow us to give clubs more coaches, so they can run bigger programs and engage more people.

Volunteers are also a big problem. It's hard to get more volunteers. The existing pool is quite small, and they get exhausted. Drop-off is high, and retention is low. So, the question is how do you activate and retain people for the long term? We've tried creating a new pathway for coaches to alleviate this, but it's tough because people often lose interest and walk away.

So, it really comes down to retention being hard, both for participants and volunteers, along with the constant need for more funding for all the programs, most of which rely on volunteers anyway.
description: Sequential relation in channel 9511.0

source_quote: At the moment, for us, working with young people I suppose particularly since post-covid, there is a larger awareness of issues around mental health for young people. Post-covid we would have supported a lot of young people who would have isolated themselves. School refusal would be the biggest issue, high anxiety, a lot of parental separation, domestic violence in the home that has impacted young people.
target_quote: So, I suppose for us, we are dealing with young people who have a greater awareness of their mental health. You know, if I go back 20 years ago, young people were more into their physical health, they didn’t consider mental health. But now for them, how they manage their mental health… I suppose some really look after it an others are not in a position to look after their mental health because they don’t have the support structure at home.

source_quote: Because of the support, recognition and encouragement provided by Rethink Ireland at a crucial early phase, the Hope & Courage Collective has flourished and grown in ways we didn't think possible. Niamh McDonald, Director of Advocacy and Community Engagement, Hope & Courage Collective
target_quote:
The main challenge is always money. We need more funding to run more programs and increase participation. This would allow us to give clubs more coaches, so they can run bigger programs and engage more people.

Volunteers are also a big problem. It's hard to get more volunteers. The existing pool is quite small, and they get exhausted. Drop-off is high, and retention is low. So, the question is how do you activate and retain people for the long term? We've tried creating a new pathway for coaches to alleviate this, but it's tough because people often lose interest and walk away.

So, it really comes down to retention being hard, both for participants and volunteers, along with the constant need for more funding for all the programs, most of which rely on volunteers anyway.
description: One or both quotes use causal language to frame the relationship.

source_quote:
The main challenge in the context of school mental health right now is bridging the gap between rising needs and the capacity of schools to respond effectively.
On one side, more young people are experiencing anxiety, stress, and low wellbeing, with issues often presenting earlier and more intensely. On the other, schools face limits in time, resources, and specialist supports — meaning teachers, though willing, often feel under-prepared and overburdened. Long waiting lists for external services like CAMHS/NEPS add pressure, leaving schools to carry much of the responsibility. The challenge is to ensure timely, preventative, and strengths-based supports that are embedded in the curriculum, guided by clear frameworks, and resourced with proper training and professional input.
target_quote: Our partnership with Rethink Ireland has been pivotal in realising our vision of an inclusive educational environment where every student has the opportunity to succeed. The support received, both financial and strategic, has been instrumental in expanding our reach and enhancing the quality of our programme, ultimately enriching the lives of our students and contributing to a more inclusive society. Professor Michael Shevlin, Director, TCPID

description: One or both quotes include tension or opposition language.

source_quote: “Mental health services are very much delivered that you see a consultant psychiatrist and then you’re offered intervention afterwards, but the two most common interventions used are CBT therapy … and medication. … There’s a gap there where we’re missing out on some of the other benefits … more recovery-based services and help people to address their lifestyle needs, to live healthier lives, have better quality of lives.”
target_quote: And it is practicing that mindfulness you know which reshapes your brain into being present, you know, reconnects with other people and not just a device. But the stories we hear from young people in terms of online bullying, grooming online… we have had some serious cases of young people being groomed online. And, I mean, it is horrific.
description: One or both quotes include tension or opposition language.

source_quote: “During Covid … people did become very isolated and moved on to the social media platforms but haven't … learned to move back and interact socially … to get people back to that community, mixing and physically being together.”
target_quote: At the moment, for us, working with young people I suppose particularly since post-covid, there is a larger awareness of issues around mental health for young people. Post-covid we would have supported a lot of young people who would have isolated themselves. School refusal would be the biggest issue, high anxiety, a lot of parental separation, domestic violence in the home that has impacted young people.
description: One or both quotes include tension or opposition language.

source_quote: “During Covid … people did become very isolated and moved on to the social media platforms but haven't … learned to move back and interact socially … to get people back to that community, mixing and physically being together.”
target_quote: We find that social media has played a significant role in the lives of young people and their mental health. You know, they are looking at their friends enjoying themselves, they’re isolated, they are looking at how they look, how they perceive themselves, body dysmorphia, depression, anxiety.
description: One or both quotes include tension or opposition language.

source_quote: The use of social media as a platform to help with better awareness of mental health with the signs on how to catch it and how to seek help when you do realize there is a problem with a friend or family member. Incorporate interactive videos on mental health to capture the attention of people, TikTok or Insta reels being the new obsession could be used rather than a post online which will get lost. We all know the likes of Pieta house and their campaign for DIL which is now global but maybe having something in the winter to boost people's spirited when the nights are dark and the mornings long.
target_quote: “Maybe in the future [we could] come up with a course or information session on just mental health and mental health awareness and education for people working in gyms. … With someone with mental health … it’s having that bit of awareness that … a personal trainer could pick up on some signs and … signpost them to the relevant service.”
description: One or both quotes include tension or opposition language.

source_quote: “I just we frame it and sometimes write it down as well that their goal for the week is to walk in the door of the gym. It’s not to do the workout … just walk in the door. And the rest usually happens by itself, but it’s just that barrier of actually getting out of the car.”
